steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The skill is Command of Evidence (Quantitative/Textual). OFT says foraging behavior reflects cost-benefit trade-offs. The decisive clue is that higher lunar intensity is positively associated with bushbaby foraging specifically because their greater vision-reliance means the light benefits them more than it does woodrats.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘If increased lunar intensity imposed the same costs on the bushbabies that it imposes on white-throated woodrats, there would be no association’: the passage never specifies that lunar intensity imposes costs on woodrats — it says foraging is negatively associated, which under OFT could mean costs outweigh benefits, but the same cost structure need not produce zero association for bushbabies. Unsupported inference. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘those advantages are likely shared by some other species not heavily reliant on vision’: the passage attributes bushbaby benefit specifically to their greater reliance on vision. Species not heavily reliant on vision would not share that same advantage. Contradicts the text’s logic. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘If increased lunar intensity creates any disadvantages for the bushbabies, those disadvantages are more than compensated for by the advantages that the bushbabies gain’: under OFT, the net positive outcome (more foraging with more light) requires that benefits exceed costs. This is the only statement that must follow from the evidence. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘their behavior should be more similar to that of white-throated woodrats than it is’: OFT explicitly allows different species to respond differently to the same circumstance based on their ecology — different responses are fully consistent with OFT, not a problem for it. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
